body.p:
In non-functional programming languages, the keywords if and then Programming ConstructsConditional Statement - if..then..else are used to tell the compiler or interpreter that a conditional statement should be evaluated.
UWE test footnote (35/40, callout I): DITA fn maps to UWE footnote/key/desc.
What may seem a bit strange to the non-functional programmer is the fact that in XQuery, if..then are evaluated as expressions.

On the one hand, this is very practical because, when used correctly, it shortens the code and thus highlights the essentials, but on the other hand it can also further contribute to the general confusion regarding cryptic XQuery code.

section.title: Example: Conditional Server App
section.p: Let's consider a simple example: We generate a JSON response on a MarkLogic webapp. Since we use this mechanism in several places in the code, it makes sense to extract the header rendering into a function.
In our request handler, a conditional statement is executed depending on the evaluation of a variable; this looks, for example, like this:
section.pre: let $name := xdmp:get-request-field('name'), $is-afternoon := xs:time(current-dateTime()) gt xs:time('12:00:00') return if ($is-afternoon) then common:wrap-response(xdmp:unquote(concat('{"greeting":"Good Afternoon! ',$name,'!"}'))) else common:wrap-response(xdmp:unquote(concat('{"greeting":"Good Morning! ',$name,'!"}')))
section.p: As a procedural programmer, I would be perfectly satisfied with this switch, but the functional programmer immediately recognizes room for optimization.
Since the conditional statement is also an expression that returns true or false, we can extract the identical function calls:
Here the abstract-thinking programmer will object that abstract logic should not be wrapped in a low-level function like xdmp:unquote.
That's true — and furthermore, the escaping with xdmp:unquote should also be packed into our function. So the code would ultimately look like this:
One can certainly debate whether the functional approach is more readable or the procedural one above.
I think every programmer has developed their own, individual, and proven programming style, which they should also maintain.
